identity.mission: HISTORY exists to make the past feel like it happened to real people — people with desires, fears, blind spots, and impossible choices — rather than to symbols in a textbook. Every event the channel covers is known to the audience at some level. The mission is to find the human story inside the event everyone thinks they know, and to tell it with enough narrative precision and emotional honesty that the viewer finishes the video feeling that they understand not just what happened, but why it could not have happened any other way. History should feel inevitable in retrospect and impossible in the moment — and the channel's job is to create that sensation in the viewer.
identity.audience: History enthusiasts and genuinely curious minds aged 20–50 who are drawn to narrative over textbook, to the drama of real human decision-making over the clean lines of retrospective analysis. They have watched documentaries, read books, listened to podcasts — and they want the format to honour their intelligence while still delivering the emotional power of great storytelling. They follow Asianometry for depth, Historia Civilis for narrative structure, and RealLifeLore for cinematic presentation — and they want a channel that combines all three at a higher level of emotional sophistication.
identity.mood: epic, cinematic, deeply human, revelatory, warm with genuine darkness, occasionally tragic, always specific
identity.forbidden: political propaganda framing that serves a contemporary political agenda, glorification of atrocities or war crimes without contextual accountability, oversimplification of events that were genuinely complex, presenting historical consensus as contested where it is not, anachronistic moral judgment that forgets the context of the period
identity.refs: Asianometry, Historia Civilis, RealLifeLore long-form documentary style, Dan Carlin's Hardcore History, David Attenborough documentary narration approach, Ken Burns documentary technique

script.tone:
The writing tone should carry the gravitas of someone who has spent their life with these stories and still finds them extraordinary — not because they are dramatic for drama's sake, but because what human beings do under pressure, fear, ambition, and love is genuinely extraordinary. The narration should move at the pace of a screenwriter and with the precision of a historian — every fact serves the story, every figure is a human being with a body temperature and a bed they slept in, not just a force in a historical process.

The narration should treat the audience as adults who can hold complexity — who can simultaneously understand why a person made a monstrous decision and not excuse it. History is not a morality play. It is more interesting than that.

The tone should move between:

Cinematic and immersive — when describing scenes and moments in real time
Historically precise and grounded — when establishing facts, dates, and documented realities
Human and specific — when describing the individuals inside the event
Controlled wonder — when the magnitude of what happened deserves to be felt
Occasionally dark and unflinching — when the events require it

Key writing characteristics:

Open in the middle of the action — the most dramatic moment, the turning point, the last day before everything changed
Use the present tense to narrate historical events — it creates immediacy
Always describe physical reality alongside political reality: what did the room smell like, what was the weather, how old was the person, how long had they been awake
Never say 'little did they know' — show instead what they believed and why
Use precise numbers and specific details — not 'thousands died' but 'at least 40,000 men in the first three days'
Let the primary sources speak: letters, diaries, contemporary accounts given directly in the narration
Always humanise the antagonist — their logic must be visible even when their actions are indefensible
End with the long consequence — not what happened, but what it meant for the people who came after
The best line of every script should be one that makes the period feel as close as this morning
script.hook: Open in the moment of maximum drama — not the beginning of the story, but the pivot point. The scene is set in present tense. The stakes are explicit. The outcome is unknown to the people inside the moment. Then pull back — 'But to understand why they were standing there, we need to go back...' The hook creates a promise: by the end of this video, you will understand not just what happened, but why it was always going to.
script.celebrities: Julius Caesar, Cleopatra, Napoleon Bonaparte, Alexander the Great, Winston Churchill, Adolf Hitler (documentary context only — the history, not glorification), Joseph Stalin, Genghis Khan, Queen Elizabeth I, Marie Curie, Nikola Tesla, Thomas Edison, Abraham Lincoln, John F. Kennedy, Martin Luther King Jr., Malcolm X, Nelson Mandela, Mahatma Gandhi, Mao Zedong, Fidel Castro, Che Guevara, Rosa Parks, Anne Frank, Harriet Tubman, Frederick Douglass, Marcus Aurelius, Hannibal Barca, Leonidas, Ramesses II, Tutankhamun, Richard the Lionheart, Saladin, Vlad the Impaler, Ivan the Terrible, Catherine the Great, Peter the Great, Louis XIV, Marie Antoinette, Robespierre, Simon Bolivar, Emperor Hirohito, Franklin D. Roosevelt, Harry Truman, Albert Einstein, Oppenheimer, Alan Turing
script.lesson: The video does not end with a lesson in the traditional motivational sense. It ends with the long consequence — what the event meant for the people who came after, for the world that was shaped by it, for the ideas that were planted or destroyed. And then, finally, a single observation about what human nature reveals in the story — not what we should do differently, but what it means that this is what we are. The ending should feel like standing at a window looking out at a world that was built by everything that just happened in the video.
script.forbidden_words: epic (overused casually), literally, basically, obviously, you guys, crazy (as a serious historical descriptor), literally insane, what followed was insane, let's be honest
script.power_words: inevitable, pivot, siege, empire, betrayal, legacy, testimony, consequence, sacrifice, ruin, rise, collapse, witness, moment, turning

visuals.vis_palette: Sepia, parchment, aged amber, deep shadow, warm blood red, faded bronze
visuals.vis_accent_color: Warm amber for dates and locations, faded gold for names of historical figures, white for direct quotations from primary sources
visuals.vis_broll_rules:
battle/war = slow pan across a battlefield landscape at dawn, weapons laid out in documentary close-up, historical maps with military movements
death/loss = single candle extinguishing, empty chair at a table set for a meal, name carved into stone
power/throne = aerial shot of a palace or fortress, throne room from the entrance looking down the length, single figure at the end
collapse/empire_fall = ruins at golden hour, columns fallen but still imposing, documents burning in courtyard
migration/movement = aerial shot of a valley with one road running through it, ships in harbour, column of figures moving across open landscape
time/era = coins from the period handled in close-up, maps of the period slowly panned, aged parchment documents
revolution = empty public square before the crowd arrives, printing press in operation, pamphlet being read by candlelight
treachery/betrayal = handshake in low light, door being closed, letter sealed and handed over, two figures and one turns away
war_council = long table with maps, hands pointing at positions, candlelight flickering over serious faces
propaganda = official portrait being painted, coins being struck with a face, public square speech from a balcony
loyalty = soldier tying armour before battle, oath-taking scene, small group standing together against a larger crowd
siege = walls seen from outside, food supplies dwindling, catapult being built in foreground
diplomacy = two groups meeting at a border, documents being exchanged, feast at long table with tense body language
religion = cathedral interior in dim light, monks at work, procession of figures in period clothing
science/discovery = old instruments in close-up, map with terra incognita regions, astronomer's notes being written
artifacts = museum-quality objects handled in documentary close-up, slow pan across aged objects on a surface
primary_source = aged parchment document, handwritten letter, official seal, aged newspaper front page
visuals.vis_aroll_rule: HISTORY does not feature living celebrities as interview subjects — all narration is driven by the narrator's voice. Visual storytelling uses: archival documentary footage, historical paintings and illustrations with slow Ken Burns pans, period artifacts in documentary close-up, historical maps animated to show movement, reconstructed environments only where clearly labelled. Expert historian interview clips from existing documentaries may be used for brief corroboration — no more than 12 seconds before narrator context resumes.
visuals.vis_forbidden: Hollywood feature film recreations used without clear attribution. Anachronistic visual elements in period footage. Colourised footage presented as original without disclosure. Modern stock footage used carelessly as a stand-in for historical footage. Any visual that glorifies atrocity without narrative accountability context. Gratuitous violence used for shock rather than historical understanding. Modern political imagery inserted into historical context to make a contemporary point. Anything that looks like a school textbook PowerPoint presentation.

mograph.triggers:
* battle = an animated tactical overhead map — armies represented as moving formations, terrain rendered in aged parchment style, movements labelled with dated arrows that trace the battle's progression in real time
* death = a single candle flame in extreme close-up, burning for a moment at full strength, then extinguished — the smoke trail rising slowly in the silence that follows
* power = camera ascending slowly from ground level toward a throne or elevated seat of authority, light increasing as height increases, scale of the room becoming apparent
* collapse = stone columns of a great structure falling in slow motion, dust rising, the aftermath of silence after the last stone has settled
* migration = animated arrows tracing paths of movement across a period map, each arrow labeled with the group or individual, terrain responding to their passage
* time = a year counter spinning forward in an aged mechanical font — slowing at significant dates, holding there for 2 seconds, then continuing
* revolution = a crowd silhouette growing from a handful of figures to a mass that fills the frame, then the frame cuts to the moment everything changed
* treachery = a dagger's shadow crossing a surface in slow motion, cast from a figure not fully in frame — the shadow reaching the intended target before the narrator names who it was
* siege = animated ring closing around a city's walls on a period map — supply lines cutting one by one, the ring tightening over days labeled with dates
* document = aged parchment unrolling slowly, text appearing as if being written in period calligraphy, key lines highlighted in warm amber as the narrator reads them
* empire = animated map showing territorial expansion — borders spreading outward from a centre, labeled by decade, colour deepening as territory grows
* legacy = a portrait of the historical figure slowly ageing and then dissolving into the landscape or consequence they created
* propaganda = coin being struck in slow motion — a face pressed into metal, the stamp landing with deliberate force
* primary_source = a letter opening, handwriting visible, key lines read by narrator — the paper's texture and age apparent in every frame
* consequence = a split screen — the world before the event and the world after, animated to show the structural differences that the event created
mograph.style:
The motion graphics language of HISTORY should feel like history made alive — aged, warm, analog, and human. The aesthetic lives between a Ken Burns documentary, an illuminated manuscript, and a museum exhibit that someone has given the gift of time and movement.

Core aesthetic rules:

* Sepia, parchment, and amber are the dominant palette — warm, aged, bearing the marks of time
* All maps should look period-appropriate — hand-drawn style, aged paper texture, compass roses, decorated borders
* Typography should feel historical — not a pastiche of old fonts, but genuinely considered period-appropriate lettering, occasionally appearing as if written by hand
* Wax seal animations mark the transition between major historical periods or chapters
* Handwritten text appearing slowly — as if being recorded by a scribe or correspondent — should be a recurring visual motif
* All historical paintings and illustrations should be treated with a subtle animation: slow zoom, gentle parallax, the Ken Burns effect — never static
* Documentary close-ups of artifacts should show patina, weight, and age — the camera should treat objects as if they are significant
* Timelines should be presented as aged scrolls or parchment strips, unrolling to reveal the next phase
* The entire visual language should convey: this happened to real people in a real world, and the world they lived in had texture, weight, and smell
* Avoid digital-looking elements — everything should have the quality of something that could have been made by hand
* Colour grading should be warm and slightly desaturated, as if everything is being viewed through decades of aged paper
mograph.text_style: Warm amber for dates and location labels. Faded gold for names of historical figures. White for direct quotations from primary sources. Period-style serif font for all document and parchment sequences. Clean condensed font for chapter titles and era markers. Text appears slowly — written rather than revealed. Maximum 5 words for impact statements. Primary source quotations may run longer — up to 2 lines — and should appear with a distinct visual treatment that codes them as direct historical record.

sound.mood:
The sound design of HISTORY should feel like the score to a cinematic epic that takes its subject seriously — grand where the story is grand, intimate where the story is intimate, and always in service of the human being at the centre of the event rather than the spectacle of the event itself.

Reference points: John Williams at his most emotionally precise, the intimacy of a solo cello for individual human moments, the full orchestral weight of a Hans Zimmer score for empire-scale consequences, the acoustic authenticity of period instruments for sequences set in specific historical eras.

For the opening — the moment of drama:

* Begin mid-action — the score should feel like it has been building before the video started
* Full orchestral texture but controlled — not chaotic, purposeful
* A clear melodic theme that will return throughout the video as the narrator's theme
* The opening should feel like the first frames of a serious historical film

For the pull-back to context and background:

* Music draws back to a smaller ensemble — strings only, or solo instrument
* The scale reduces as the human scale of the story is re-established
* Tempo slows slightly — this is the breath before the story begins to build again

For battle and consequence sequences:

* Full orchestra, building in controlled waves
* Never chaotic — the chaos of war should be expressed in the editing, not the score
* Music should feel like the weight of inevitability, not the excitement of violence
* Use silence before the final blow — then sound returns

For intimate human moments (a letter, a final decision, a private conversation):

* Solo cello, violin, or piano — minimal, human-scale
* The music should feel like the sound of one person's experience, not a nation's
* Allow space between notes — what is not played matters as much as what is

For the long consequence and ending:

* Music gradually returns to the full orchestral theme from the opening
* But now it feels different — weighted by everything that has happened
* A version of triumph that acknowledges its cost
* Music ends before the video does — the final words are delivered into silence or near-silence

Avoid:

* Drum-heavy battle music that makes war feel exciting rather than consequential
* Anachronistic modern sounds in period settings
* Generic epic trailer music that treats history as spectacle
* Triumphant endings that do not acknowledge the cost of what came before
* Music that tells the viewer how to feel rather than creating the space for them to feel it
sound.ambient: Period-appropriate ambient layers: quill on parchment, distant crowd in an ancient city, wind across a battlefield aftermath, fire in a great hall, oars in water, city construction at the height of an empire
sound.silence: Music withdraws completely for 3–5 seconds at the moment a primary source is read directly — a letter, a recorded speech, a final testimony. The words of the actual historical figure deserve silence. Music returns slowly after the reading ends. This rule applies to every primary source quotation in every video.
sound.swells: First orchestral swell at the moment the narrative returns to the opening dramatic scene — the viewer now understands how the people arrived at this point. Second swell at the moment of greatest consequence — the decision that changed everything has been made, and now the world must live with it. Final movement: the long consequence — music returns to the theme but in a minor or more complex key, acknowledging what was built and what was lost.
sound.extra: For sequences set in specific historical periods, layer in subtle period-appropriate ambient sound beneath the score — the city sounds of ancient Rome, the shipyard sounds of 15th century Venice, the cannon aftermath of a 19th century battlefield. These sounds should be barely audible under the music and narration, but should be clearly perceptible when the viewer focuses. They make the period feel physically real. All primary source quotations — letters, diaries, speeches — should be delivered by the narrator in a slightly different audio treatment: a subtle increase in room reverb to suggest that these words were spoken in a real space long ago.

editing.edit_pacing:
Opening dramatic hook = immersive and cinematic. Cut every 4–7 seconds. Each cut adds a new layer to the scene — a face, a landscape, a weapon, a date. The viewer should feel dropped into a moment already in progress. Never slow enough to lose tension, never fast enough to lose comprehension.

Context and background sections = measured and building. Cut every 7–12 seconds. These sections use more historical images, maps, and paintings — the slower pace honours their weight. Use slow Ken Burns pans across paintings and photographs rather than rapid cuts. Every cut should introduce a new fact or a new human detail.

Narrative and human story sections = warm, medium pacing. Cut every 5–8 seconds. Alternate between landscape footage, artifact close-ups, map animations, and the occasional archival photograph with slow pan. These sections should feel like a documentary building the portrait of a person under pressure.

Battle, revolution, and consequence sequences = dynamic but never chaotic. Cut every 2–5 seconds. Use tactical map animations for strategic overview, then cut to human scale — the individual in the battle, the single letter written the night before, the specific moment of decision. The sequence should feel both epic and intimate.

Long consequence and ending = slowest and most reflective. 12–18 seconds per shot. One idea per cut. The viewer should feel the full weight of what they have just witnessed — not summarised for them, but given space to feel.

General rules:

* Use a slow, deliberate establishing shot for every new location or time period — let the viewer orient before the narration continues
* Every named historical figure gets a portrait or visual representation on first mention
* Dates should appear on screen as they are spoken — in amber, aged font, lower left
* Major structural transitions (time period changes, geographic shifts) use a slow dissolve or film burn — never a hard cut
* Maps should appear for any geographical reference — always animated, never static
editing.edit_text_rules: Dates appear in warm amber in the lower left corner every time they are spoken. Location names appear in the lower right, in the same aged font. All named historical figures receive a lower third on first appearance: NAME / TITLE OR ROLE / YEARS (e.g. JULIUS CAESAR / CONSUL OF ROME / 100–44 BC). Primary source quotations appear on screen in their entirety as they are read — aged parchment backing, period-appropriate font, distinguished from all other text by their distinct visual style. Chapter or era markers appear as full-screen transitions between major sections — parchment texture, title in period font. Maximum 6 words for impact overlays.
editing.edit_end: Final 25–30 seconds: return to the landscape or place where the story began — but now, seen through the lens of the long consequence. Narrator delivers the final observation about what human nature reveals in this story. A date or a legacy statement appears on screen. Music reaches its most complete and weighted version. Then silence, or near-silence, for the final words. Channel watermark appears quietly in the lower right. 4 seconds of held final shot. Slow fade to black. The ending should feel like leaving a site of historical significance.
editing.edit_special:
Every HISTORY video should include one dedicated 'map sequence' — a custom animated period map tracing the geographic movement of the story. This should animate in real time as the narrator describes movements, decisions, and consequences. The map style is always: aged parchment texture, hand-drawn cartographic style, amber and sepia palette, animated formation movements and border changes.

For significant primary sources (letters, diaries, last words, speeches), use a dedicated visual treatment: full screen, aged parchment, the text appearing slowly as if being written or read for the first time. The narrator's voice should slow slightly during these sections — the words deserve their full weight.

For any historical figure who is well documented, include one personal detail that is almost never cited in general coverage — something from their private correspondence, their recorded eating habits, their specific fear or vanity. A single human detail that makes the historical figure suddenly real. This detail should appear approximately 30% into the video and should be the moment the viewer's relationship to the historical figure changes from knowing a name to feeling a life.

narrator.persona: A historian who has spent a lifetime with these stories and still finds them extraordinary — not because they are dramatic for their own sake, but because what human beings do to each other and for each other under the weight of history is genuinely one of the most remarkable things that exists. Speaks with controlled wonder. Never performs emotion — just tells the truth of what happened with enough precision and care that the emotion is already inside the facts. The voice of someone who believes that understanding history is one of the most important things a person can do, and who communicates that belief through the quality of the telling rather than by saying it.
narrator.role: The narrator is the single thread of historical consciousness running through the entire video. They open the story, establish context, humanise the figures, present the evidence, build toward consequence, and deliver the final reflection. All other voices — historian clips, contemporary accounts, primary source readings — exist within the frame the narrator creates. The narrator speaks not as a commentator watching history, but as someone who has lived inside it long enough to understand why it could not have happened any other way.
narrator.doc_structure: Narrator opens in the moment of maximum drama — present tense, specific detail, stakes established immediately. Then pulls back: 'But to understand why they were standing there, we need to go back [timeframe].' Context section: world conditions, the major figures, what everyone believed was about to happen and why they were wrong. First significant event: narrated in real time, with human detail. Primary source quotation — narrator delivers letter, diary entry, or speech. Stakes escalate. Second pivotal moment: the decision that changes everything. Historian or expert clip provides corroboration. Narrator re-enters with consequence — what that decision meant for every person in the story. Final section: the long consequence — what the event built, what it destroyed, what it set in motion. Closing reflection on what human nature reveals. Final primary source quotation or the last recorded words. Music. Silence.

shorts.formula:
Every Short is built around one historical fact that sounds impossible until you understand the context — then it sounds inevitable. Open with the fact. No setup. Then 20–30 seconds of the minimal context needed to make it land. Then the revelation of what it actually meant. Then silence.

▸ SECONDS 0–3: The historical fact stated as flatly and specifically as possible. A date, a name, a number, a decision. Large text on screen.
▸ SECONDS 3–12: The minimum context needed: who, when, what the world believed at the time.
▸ SECONDS 12–40: The mechanism — what actually happened, why it mattered, what made it possible.
▸ SECONDS 40–55: The consequence or the revelation — what it changed forever.
▸ SECONDS 55–60: One final line. Silence. Text. Fade.

The Short ends not with resolution but with the feeling that history is closer than the viewer thought.
shorts.hook: First frame: the most impossible-sounding historical fact, stated with complete specificity — a real number, a real date, a real name. No framing. The fact IS the hook. The viewer's disbelief is the reason they stay. Large text. Aged font. The visual codes it as real, not entertainment.
shorts.visuals: Documentary and archival in feel. Historical paintings with subtle Ken Burns effect. Period maps animated. Aged document textures. Sepia and amber palette maintained from long-form. Text in period-appropriate style. Cuts slower than other channels' Shorts — 5–8 seconds per visual — because historical content requires orientation. Film grain slightly reduced for mobile readability. Every visual should be unambiguously historical — this should look like a serious history Short, not a dramatisation.
shorts.music: Cinematic and orchestral — the same register as long-form but condensed to a single melodic thread. No trending audio. During the central historical fact reveal, music withdraws almost completely. Returns as the consequence unfolds. Ends quietly — strings only, barely audible — during the final line. The Short should feel like a historical film's opening title sequence compressed into 60 seconds.

thumbnail.thumb_concept:
Primary concept: a dramatic historical image — a portrait, a battlefield landscape, an artifact, a map detail — treated with cinematic colour grading and composition. The image should feel like a film poster for the most important moment of the event. One face if the story centres on a figure; one symbolic image if the story centres on an event. The composition should feel like the moment before everything changed.

Colour palette:
sepia + amber = historical depth, documentary weight
blood red + black = war, revolution, assassination
parchment + shadow = investigation, hidden history, document-based stories
faded bronze + white = empire, legacy, civilisation at its height
cold grey + amber = collapse, aftermath, the weight of consequence

Style: cinematic, serious, the visual equivalent of a history documentary poster or a serious book cover. The thumbnail should immediately communicate that this is rigorous, emotional, and genuinely worth 20 minutes of the viewer's time.
thumbnail.thumb_font: Bebas Neue for era-scale events and empire-level stories. Period-appropriate serif for more intimate historical narratives. All caps. Warm white or aged gold. Never overlaid on the most important part of the historical image.
thumbnail.thumb_text_formula: Create the sensation that the viewer is missing the real story of something they thought they already knew. Examples: THE NIGHT BEFORE IT FELL / HE KNEW IT WAS OVER / WHAT THE HISTORY BOOKS SKIP / THE LAST DECISION / BEFORE IT ALL CHANGED / THE COST OF WINNING. Never reveal the conclusion — only imply the weight of what is inside. Use specific time language: 'the night before', 'one hour later', 'the day it ended'.
thumbnail.thumb_title_formula:
The [last/true/hidden] [hours/days/decision] of [historical figure or event]
What history [forgets/skips/never tells you] about [event]
The [decision/moment/letter] that [changed/ended/started] everything
Why [historical figure] made the [decision that defined their legacy]
[Historical event]: the [human story] nobody tells
The [night/day/year] [historical figure] understood what was happening
How [empire/civilisation/decision] actually [rose/fell/was made]
The truth about [battle/revolution/discovery] history simplified

All titles under 70 characters. Lead with the human or dramatic element, not the date or formal historical name. Never use a question mark. The title should make the viewer feel that the standard account of this event is insufficient — and that this video has the real story.
thumbnail.thumb_forbidden: Modern stock footage used as historical stand-in. Hollywood actor faces used as historical figures. Anachronistic visual elements. Bright colours that break the period aesthetic. Multiple unrelated images crammed into one thumbnail. Text that makes the video sound like conspiracy content. Any visual that glorifies violence or atrocity without compositional accountability. Clipart or illustration-style representations of historical events. Anything that looks like a school history project.

seo.description:
SEO Package — HISTORY channel

Searchable terms to include naturally:
history, documentary, true story, historical events, world history, biography, what really happened, historical documentary, explained, hidden history, ancient history, modern history, the truth about

Description template:
In [period/year], [subject or event] [happened in a way most accounts miss]. In this documentary, we go inside [the specific human story] — the people, the decisions, and the consequences that [standard accounts] leave out.

This is not a textbook summary. It is the real story.

🎬 More historical documentaries: [link]

00:00 – [Opening dramatic moment]
[timestamps for each chapter]

#history #documentary #{specific event or figure} #{period} #truestory

Description should be written with the gravitas of serious historical work — not academic, but authoritative. First two lines should make the viewer feel that they are about to understand something they thought they already knew. Use specific dates, names, and places in the description — specificity establishes credibility.
seo.tags: history documentary, world history, historical events, what really happened, true story, ancient history, modern history, historical documentary, explained, hidden history, biography, historical figures
seo.cta: If history has always felt like something that happened to other people in another world — subscribe. We make one documentary a week that puts you inside the room where the decisions were made. Leave a comment with the historical event or figure you want explored next.
